project_overview.project_name: South Sudan Resilient Agricultural Livelihoods Project (RALP)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To strengthen capacity of farmers and their organizations and improve agricultural production
project_overview.beneficiaries: 140,000 direct beneficiaries including smallholder farmers, local youth, women, community resource persons, and agricultural extension staff

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - South Sudan is classified as a Fragile, Conflict and Violence (FCV) country with ongoing localized conflict, intercommunal violence, and displacement affecting all project areas

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Capacity Building in Good Agricultural Practices
key_components.description: Formation and strengthening of farmer organizations; improving farming knowledge and skills through training in climate-smart agriculture, good agricultural practices, and extension services. Includes infrastructure development for farmer organization centers and capacity building for extension agents.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Investment Support for Improved Agricultural Production
key_components.description: Increasing access to food for households facing acute food insecurity (IPC-3+); increasing availability of quality seeds through community-led quality declared seed production and farmer-led seed enterprises; enhancing access to technology and mechanization through provision of agricultural inputs, equipment, and tools.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management and Technical Assistance
key_components.description: Project management and coordination through a Project Coordination Unit within MAFS; technical assistance and capacity building support to Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security; monitoring and evaluation; third-party monitoring; grievance redress mechanism; and environmental and social safeguard implementation.

Contingent component to provide immediate response to eligible crises or emergencies through reallocation of undisbursed funds from other components. Includes preparation of Contingent Emergency Response Manual and Emergency Action Plan.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security (MAFS) as the lead implementing agency with direct contracting of Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) as the lead technical partner for implementation of components 1, 2, and sub-component 3.2
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) comprising Minister of Finance and Planning, Minister of Agriculture and Food Security, and Governors of participating states; Project Coordination Unit (PCU) within MAFS for day-to-day management; FAO Project Implementation Units (PIUs) in six field locations; coordination through Partnership for Recovery and Resilience (PfRR) with bilateral donors, UN organizations, and African Development Bank
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for Investment Project Financing; Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared; procurement methods include international competitive bidding, national competitive bidding, shopping, single source selection, and direct contracting; direct contracting with FAO as UN agency; prior and post-review thresholds established; procurement plan updated biannually
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Baseline survey within first three months; mid-term review and end-of-project surveys; semi-annual progress reports and interim financial reports; third-party monitoring agent (TPMA) to conduct independent monitoring every six months; Geo-Enabled Monitoring System (GEMS) for data collection; results framework with PDO and intermediate indicators; M&E specialist and knowledge management specialist in PCU

identified_risks.risk_description: Atrocities, widespread violence against civilians, and conflict at local level are common; new armed groups continue to emerge; inadequate governance capacity for neutral, accountable, transparent service delivery; slow materialization of peace agreement reforms; doubts about long-term viability of peace process; high possibility of large-scale violence
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Transparent and inclusive planning process; fostering social cohesion through participatory planning with special consideration for women, ultra-poor, and marginalized households; local conflict mitigation training; flexibility in implementation to make adjustments; strengthened monitoring for early risk identification

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation and depreciation of local currency; significant variance between parallel and official exchange rates; increased prices and low incomes reducing purchasing power of poorest and most vulnerable; potential for increased violence as people compete for resources and food
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Coordination with government, other donors, and UN to leverage food assistance for IPC-3+ households; incurring most project expenditures in US$ through supply contracts to implementing agencies; direct payments to suppliers; monitoring of exchange rates

identified_risks.risk_description: Fragmented agricultural policy and regulatory framework could impact long-term sustainability of investments; however, would not impede project implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: More strategic and coordinated approach among key partners through coordination platforms such as PfRR; sub-component 3.2 will contribute to improved policy and regulatory reforms that can be adopted by government

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: RALP is among first to return to government implementation; protracted conflict, violence, and economic meltdown have eroded management and technical capacity of MAFS and CAD; budgetary lapses lead to significant salary arrears for civil servants; low capacity of local-level structures and farmers; difficult access to project areas due to localized conflict and high mistrust among communities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Focus on capacity building, training, skills enhancement, and hands-on support to MAFS and CAD staff and farmers; conflict management and resolution training; close collaboration with relevant partners and local authorities; flexibility to withdraw from insecure areas and restore operations when security improves; World Bank and IMF support for public financial management systems

identified_risks.risk_description: Agriculture sector subject to risks from climate change, disease outbreak, pests, and conflict; existing in-country systems not equipped to fully manage these risks; implementation through agencies with experience in FCV environments but still subject to technical uncertainties
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Contracting with FAO with demonstrated competence in technical agricultural activities; introduction of resilient good quality seeds that can withstand drought, flood, and heat; phased implementation starting in few locations to test approach; frequent supervision backed by TPMA; work with research agencies and MAFS

identified_risks.risk_description: Access restrictions, weak institutional capacity, weak governance and accountability systems, rising inflation, weak local currency; decentralized activities under components 1 and 2 at risk of not benefiting intended beneficiaries; risk of diversion of resources to unintended beneficiaries; difficulty in coordinating, supervising, and monitoring multiple activities in diverse locations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust financial management and procurement system with regular external audits; contracting FAO as lead technical agency with appropriate expertise and adequate local access; segregated project accounts and financial statements; monthly bank reconciliation; internal audit reviews at least once yearly; annual external audit by National Audit Chamber; third-party monitoring; technology-enabled monitoring using GEMS; direct payments to FAO to mitigate risks of holding funds in designated account

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall FCV situation with potential interrelation with project including IDPs, prevalence of sexual and gender-based violence, specific needs of culturally distinct communities; decades of war have engendered culture of mistrust, revenge killing, and competition over resources; even minor voluntary land giving needs sensitive handling; low capacity of local communities for sustainable natural resource management; dysfunctional national legal and institutional arrangements for environmental health and safety compliance; low capacity in controlling and monitoring environmental performance
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: PCU effective implementation of Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF); TPMA regular monitoring of ESMF compliance; building ESMF capacity of farmers, project staff, and participating implementation partners; feedback mechanisms for meaningful consultations; nuanced communication and social messaging to reinforce peaceful cooperation and unity; systematic reinforcement against GBV; promotion of greater interaction and dialogue among diverse ethnic groups

identified_risks.risk_description: GBV perpetration driven by underlying norms, economic structures, and power imbalances between men and women; ongoing conflict, displacement, and pervasive insecurity have worsened GBV incidence; increased exposure of women and girls to varying forms of violence; contextual risks from FCV context and pervasive social norms; project-related risks from changes in gender inequitable household dynamics, exposure to sexual exploitation and abuse by contractors, risks linked to women's mobility and participation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Full suite of GBV/SEAH risk mitigation measures including GBV action plan, dedicated GBV/SEAH specialist, safety audits, awareness raising and training, accountability and response framework, reporting and response protocol, TPMA oversight, support services for survivors; detailed GBV assessment in project areas upon site selection; proactive mitigation measures; specific GRM systems to capture GBV issues; training for men, women, girls, and youth

identified_risks.risk_description: Perennial risk from delayed rains, excessive rains, drought, high temperatures; climate change impacts on agriculture and food security; vulnerability of agricultural production to climate shocks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Careful monitoring of weather models to guide and advise farmers; adaptive tests for seed varieties resilient to drought and diseases; community-based extension services and early warning systems; promotion of gender-sensitive and climate-smart management practices; technologies introduced by project to help farming families manage risks better

identified_risks.risk_description: Insecurity remains main risk to achievement of PDO; can constrain movement of implementation partners and stall subproject implementation; delays in transportation of construction materials; security risk to project staff, especially females and those working for contracted NGOs; beneficiaries at risk from violent looting, cattle raiding, being caught between warring parties, attacks in highly insecure areas
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Standard operating procedures for managing security risks; close collaboration with county, payam, and boma authorities to increase area security; frank consultations with participating communities about risks and mitigation preferences; security training to staff; security reviews of procedures and physical structures; upgraded physical infrastructure such as metal doors; increased communications including satellite phones; procedures for responding to security incidents; adoption and implementation of Security Management Plan; operation in more secure areas with procedures for sudden deterioration; suspension of activities in areas with insecurity; consideration of restructuring options if security situation deteriorates significantly

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High Environmental and Social Risk Classification
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-wide locally based GRM proportionate to potential risks and impacts, established prior to commencement of project activities; GRM specifically for direct and contracted workers; specific channels for safe, confidential reporting of SEAH and GBV cases; procedures for managing reporting and responding to GBV/SEAH cases developed as part of GBV/SEAH action plan; FAO's established Grievance Mechanism for beneficiaries with feedback desks, suggestions boxes, toll-free hotlines, and community Accountability Committees; World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel available for project-affected communities
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender-responsive approach to climate-smart agriculture; assessment of gender division of labor and gendered benefits; technologies based on needs and interests of both female and male farmers, reducing time and labor for women, accessible and affordable to both; special efforts to promote recognition of women's role in agriculture; equal access to agricultural inputs, services, and benefits; minimization of GBV risks; training on women's importance in agriculture, women empowerment, equal opportunities, girls' education, GBV management; encouragement of women and youth inclusion in farmer organizations with at least 30 percent of key management committee positions held by women; formation of women and youth-specific interest groups; interactive approaches suited for women with low literacy levels; training in proximity to communities at times women can attend; childcare facilities as needed; special attention to youth inclusion and participation; gender-disaggregated data collection and monitoring

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity as core principle; aims to reduce conflict especially over access to resources and nurture social harmony and promote peace at local level; participatory community-based beneficiary identification process to prevent elite capture; inclusive planning process with special consideration for women, ultra-poor, and marginalized households; local conflict mitigation training; transparent and inclusive community engagement framework; social cohesion fostered through participatory planning; conflict-sensitive community mobilization approach; frank consultations with communities about risks of violence and mitigation preferences; positioning of storage infrastructure and community organization for protection of fields and facilities determined through community consultation; support for homestead production where insecurity precludes farm field cultivation; coordination with local authorities to increase area security; nuanced communication and social messaging to reinforce peaceful cooperation and unity among diverse ethnic groups
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan is one of the poorest countries in the world with 82 percent of population designated as poor; ranks second to last in World Bank's Human Capital Index 2020; approximately 6.48 million people (54 percent of total population) experience food security crisis (IPC Phase 3 or worse); 1.8 million suffer from acute malnutrition; nearly 3.8 million people displaced (1.6 million IDPs and 2.26 million international refugees); approximately 7.5 million people in need of humanitarian assistance throughout the year; economy collapsed in 2016 with slow recovery; GDP contracted 11.2 percent (2016), 6.9 percent (2017), 3.5 percent (2018); per capita income US$375 in 2019; high inflation and currency depreciation; heavily reliant on foreign aid; real incomes declined 50 percent since 2013; food prices increased 489 percent between May 2016 and May 2018; 52 percent of population spending more than 75 percent of income on food; COVID-19 pandemic, lower oil prices, adverse weather, flooding, and desert locust infestation aggravating fragile dynamics; economy projected to contract 3.4 percent in FY20/21; fiscal financing gap estimated at 4 percent of GDP; prices of basic commodities surged 30-150 percent in 2020; half of households reported fall in income since pandemic start; 45 percent unable to buy main staple food; volatile political environment encouraging budget allocation for security reducing fiscal space for public investments
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Ongoing localized conflict at sub-national level fueled by intercommunal violence, state capture of resources, power struggles among ruling political groups, distrust of government, dispute over representation, and legacy of unresolved grievances; communal violence driven by disputes over access to natural resources including land and water, food and services; traditional cattle culture and rustling cycles remain source of conflict and displacement; insecurity restricts access to project sites; COVID-19 restricting travel limiting World Bank supervision; project will operate in more secure areas with flexibility to withdraw from insecure areas; phased approach contingent on security situation; security management plan adopted and implemented; security training for staff; security reviews of procedures and physical structures; upgraded physical infrastructure for security; increased communications including satellite phones; procedures for responding to security incidents; coordination with World Bank corporate security; standard operating procedures for managing security risks developed by UN agencies and NGOs; close collaboration with local authorities to increase security; community consultation on security risks and mitigation preferences
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Nearly 3.8 million people displaced as of August 2020 (1.6 million IDPs and 2.26 million international refugees); project targets IDPs and returnees as vulnerable groups; project will ensure IDPs are not left behind and actively participate and benefit from project interventions; innovative and gender-sensitive strategies to ensure access to land and security of tenure for all targeted groups including IDPs through social contract with Boma and Payam level leadership; project will work with local traditional authorities to ensure land use rights assigned equitably; special support for households facing acute food insecurity (IPC-3+) to improve immediate access to food; coordination with South Sudan Social Safety Net Project (SSSNP) and other relevant initiatives focusing on stabilizing households facing food insecurity; project recognizes roles and responsibilities being reshaped by effects of armed conflict; special attention to inclusion and participation of IDPs in farmer organizations and project interventions
